Alright, we're into step four already. And this is a simple, straightforward one, but it's very, very important. I want you to do a master list of features and benefits. Now I'm going to explain to you the difference between features and benefits. These are essential building blocks of all ads. They will be the essence of your bullets, the essence of the sales pitch. They'll be part of your headline probably. They'll be part of your email, subject line and also your emails. If you do speeches, they will be part of this. So you need to understand the difference of features and benefits. Basically, I've written this out, features are the physical manifestation of things. The things that actually go bang when you drop them or crash. The, when I say duck here, it means that if somebody threw the feature across the room, you would have to duck because it's a physical thing. But it's also characteristics, titles and plans. So a feature would be a, let's say you have a book. The features would be, it's an actual hardcover book with pages printing and it's got a title. These are the characteristics of it. The plans that you make the features, you may say, I have a business plan. Now that's just a feature. You may say, I have the plans to build a house on a hillside. This is just the building block for the house. The plans are not the house. It's having a set of plans for a house doesn't keep the rain off your head when a storm hits. The actual house will. So the features are the things, the building blocks, what we call it. The benefits are what are attached to the features. So most ads only spell out the features. You may say, I have a new book out that will teach you how to do blank, blank, blank. And it's 1995 and you can pick it up on Amazon. Those are all features because why I would buy that book is not because I'm looking for a book or I have 1995 to spend and I need to spend it somewhere or that I just can't wait to get on Amazon and order something. None of that matters to me. These are features. Now these are important features, but they are not the thing that is going to start to affect me personally. Those are what we call benefits. And I've written here yummy, ooh-la-la, and warm fuzzies. In other words, the benefits of the book on blank, blank, blank. Let's say the book is on retiring 10 years early even if you haven't saved a penny yet. So the benefits would be my emotional state. Wow, I feel better now because I was freaked out. Now I'm not going to be freaked out. The consequences that were coming at me fast and furious are now not going to be coming at me. Those features of the book for 1995 on Amazon are now have benefits attached to them. I'm going to feel better. I now have a way to get out of the bad situation I'm in and get into a good situation. So we're talking about the effects, the consequences, what the features do for you, what this means for you, their results. So I want you to list down everything you have. I don't care if you've got a service. Say you're a plumber and you show up somewhere and you bring your toolkit and you will fix leaks. Yeah, let's just go that far. You're a plumber who will show up with his equipment and fix leaks. Those are features of what you do. What I have as a customer is I have a leak I need fixed. I got water running all over the place in the hallway. I've got a ruined carpet. I've got no idea what to do. I don't even know how to turn off the main water to the house. So the benefits to me are going to be the leak is going to be stopped. I might learn something about how the water actually gets into the house and you're going to present me with a solution to fixing the problem, which is I may need a new faucet. I may need pipes replaced. I may just need some plumbers tape wrapped around some kind of leak somewhere. So the benefits are very different than the features. Let's say the simple writing course here that with the simple writing system comes with online access to a membership site. It comes with maybe a workbook. There are DVDs. There is me standing here talking to you. These are all features. None of this addresses your needs. Your needs are you want to be able to write all of the sales messaging and ads that you're ever going to write. You want to do it quickly and easily and you want to feel confident that no matter what situation you're in in your business, you will be able to write what is needed to be written to get things done, to have customers or to have prospects become customers, to have the customers stay happy, to have the product do what it's supposed to do. So make a list of all the features that you have and then attach benefits to them. Often a feature will have multiple benefits. Let's say a car that goes from zero to 60 in 60 in 10 seconds. Zero to 60 in 10 seconds a car. The car is a feature. It's zero to 60 is nothing in and of itself. It's a statistic. However, if the benefits for this would be, hey, a new car, which is something I've really wanted. It's a little bit of a status symbol. I will be able to get around a lot better than I did when I was having to rely on the bus system. And zero to 60. Are you kidding me? That's fast. That means that I can race other cars on the road. That means I can get wherever I want to go. If I'm in a situation, I can get out of there as fast as possible. All of these other benefits get attached to this. So this is going to teach you the difference between features and benefits. And of course, if you're in the coaching program, your hand will be held as you go through this. So make a list of the features and then make a list of attaching at least one benefit to each feature. If you have more benefits, even better. Pay attention to the difference between benefits and features, which is the physical manifestation of features versus the mostly effects, the consequences, and the emotional stuff behind benefits. Okay. For the question and answer segment, there's only going to be a couple of questions. This is, in my opinion, while every part of the simple riding system is really important, if they had to pick one thing, if you could really just understand one part of this only and thus build from there, this section would be it. Now in a lot of ways, even though John's lecture was kind of short, it doesn't lend itself to explaining it on and on and on and on. So what we're going to try to do is give you some examples, some different ways I've learned from simple riding system coaches. They recommend this is done. And then get some feedback from John. This is, if you're involved in the coaching program, the first time you post your exercise, I'll almost guarantee most people don't nail this the first time. It's difficult. The closer you are to the product of the service, the more it's your baby. Sometimes these things blur in your own mind, but if you just, another example, stick to the process. And if you're working with the coach, take their advice, be open to it. You're going to end up with most, or if not most of, a lot of the building blocks that you're then going to actually put into your sales copy, whether it's a letter or a video or whatever. Even veteran copywriters with a lot of experience screw this part up because the difference between a feature and a benefit is sometimes subtle. So you need to get this down. Once you get it down, as Stan said, it's kind of launching pad for a lot of other things that are going to happen. Okay. Some people involved in the program are selling physical products. Some are selling services. Sure. It's a mixture of both. There's a service with physical product. So let's just a few more examples of features of a, let's say of a physical product that are a little bit easier, but let's talk just features of a service that we could maybe give some more examples to help people. Well, like an accountant. An accountant has an office, which is a feature. He works five days a week, you know, 50 weeks a year, which is a feature. He has, he offers bookkeeping services, which is a feature. He will help you get your taxes filed on time, which is a feature. He can give advice on tax structures or minimizing your taxes or whatever, which is a feature closing in on a benefit. Now the benefits to all of those, which is he may have an office downtown in the city you live in, as opposed to dealing with somebody who is far out of town or doesn't have an office at all. So if you really need to sit down with your accountant and talk this out because you're not very good at numbers, you screw things up and you just got a big shoe box with all your receipts in it, then him having an office where you can go to and sit down, that becomes a benefit to you. The fact that he works 50 weeks a year can be a huge benefit if your current accountant or the last five accountants you've had only works six months a year and then take all this time off. What else did I say? The filing things on time is a feature. However, if this has been a problem for you and you've actually wound up being penalized for this or it has cost you in other ways, then that becomes the benefit is you will never pay a penalty again to the IRS. You will never get into that situation where you're scrambling to get your stuff done later. Bookkeeping I think was another. I just went through this myself. I mean, I have a bookkeeper now who comes in every month or so. They just clean up the books. Oh my God, it's just, it's stunning what the advantages are. I know where I am at all times. My profit and loss statements are up to date. We can pull them up with a couple of clicks on one of the programs. It's like what used to be a big mystery that always had a vague aura of anxiety over it. How much have I really made? Where did the money go? It's now gone. It was all because the bookkeeping just happened. It's nothing I'm doing. It's just the feature was the bookkeeping. The results of the bookkeeping were I sleep better at night. I can answer any question anybody has about my current financial situation. That just makes for a better life all over. Good example. You do realize for our business we've been doing that for eight years. I'm talking about my side of the business. Okay, I'm just going on the record in case. That was some good examples of, again, a service where, I'm going to give David Garfinkel, the first one I heard say this, so I'll give him the credit for it, is if it's something you're really interested in, like you're the accountant and you hang out with accountants, the thing that most excites you about the business or the offer, and this is equally true if it's a physical product, but you're often excited about features. It's cool that I do this thing or it's cool that there's this knob on this. Quick books would be a good example to stick with the accountant thing. We do quick books. To you, that means all kinds of things such as just I was talking about, easy access to all the numbers, a couple of clicks to get to find out anything you wanted to do. For somebody who doesn't understand what quick books is or what they advantage of, that's a feature. A lot of times the accountant will say, I have quick books. The prospect says, yes, so what? It's like, I don't care what program you use. No, you don't understand. Then you start to explain it. Quick books means you can do XX, YZ, and all this other stuff. Then the prospect goes, oh, I want to do that. That's when you're talking about the benefits. Okay. In fact, I'm going to skip a couple of questions because you just stumbled onto what I think is the key phrase is to help people get clear on that. What you just said was so what? And I have found I'll just kind of, a technique I have seen work, I use this all the time and I've recommended others do it and it seems to help a lot of people get organized, is you mentioned in the lecture, like the two columns. The way you should begin your exercise is what John said. You just, on the top of the piece of paper, you write down the word, the feature, and on the left column at the top, feature here, benefit here, and then you start going down and you should be able to line them up. That there's a knob on it, you might think that knob is cool, but what benefit does that provide? Sometimes you'll do the reverse. I think sometimes you'll know you provide this benefit and you may need to then actually kind of fill that form in in reverse order. Well, how do I do that? Because maybe again, that's something that you may need to explain in your sales message that you're just thinking is trivial or obvious. And sometimes you'll, okay, so there is a feature that provides this benefit. But to me, I'm kind of going along around to the so what is the key question. Yes. You can put something forward as a, this is a benefit. And if your prospect could look at you and say, well, so what? Yes. It's probably a feature. Yeah, I think that's it. Because that's what you're really trying to solve with benefits. Where if there's a, if it needs more explaining, and you're doing this exercise, if you write down something you think is a benefit and then you could ask yourself, well, why is that a benefit? If you have more to say, if you have more to write, keep going. Like allow yourself to add a third column right now during the exercise and say, well, maybe this thing is, I don't care what the heading is right now. Keep working to the right. What were the benefits of that thing? What points did you make when you were explaining so what? And then really you could do that again. That's really good. And I think at some point you will run out of things to say. In words, you will not be going 100 columns across the piece of paper. You'll hit a point where, okay, this is, it has this, it provides this benefit. You will sleep better at night knowing that your taxes are under control, the IRS is going to come for you. If that is not appealing, like, okay, then you're done. Like maybe this, you're not talking to your avatar anymore. If the person has more, keeps saying so what to the point where you think, oh, this is my avatar would understand why this. Does that make a reason to start this? Yes. Take, for example, writing ads. I always think of it as two people talking and I say, I will teach you how to write an ad. And it's like, you do actually this moment, you do this, you're waiting for them to respond. You're embracing their response. And if they just look at you and go, yeah, and then you have presented a feature. Knowing how to write ads doesn't mean anything until you explain what it does. This means you will never have to hire a freelancer. People will actually know who you are, how to find you and how much your stuff is, and they'll know how to pay for it and get it. And then you imagine the person going, oh, oh, oh. Then it suddenly becomes manifest in their mind of what's going on. So the best thing to do is you just start writing them out because you will find that you have some features in your benefit column. You may find you have some benefits in your features column. That's okay. Even professional writers do this. If you want, have little, you know, if you're doing it in a word doc, you can just highlight it and move it back and forth and do all this stuff. Sometimes a single feature will have dozens of benefits. Sometimes a feature has just one benefit, but that benefit may be the main thing that carries your entire ad. That may be the benefit that just seals the deal. Sometimes you have 12 benefits and only one of those benefits are the one that seals the deal. Sometimes you'll have 100 benefits and none of them seal the deal, but it helps explain things to the prospect. So they're going, oh, I get it. I get it now. Oh, okay. So if you can start thinking about the way the features are things that you may be really excited about, but they don't mean anything. It's like a seminar. A lot of clients I've had put on seminars and they say, we're doing a seminar and it's in Chicago and it's in the end of January and it's going to be on how to write ads. And people just go, okay, you know, the seminar, those are all features. Nobody wants to go to a seminar. They may want to go to a seminar if it's on a subject that they're interested in, but still they ain't going to close the deal. However, if that subject that they're going to learn at the seminar is something that is so critical to their success, say how to write ads in 15 minutes and have them work really well, then you may say, okay, I will go to the seminar. However, the seminar is just a vehicle for presenting the information. So it could be you write a book. So instead of a seminar, you have a book or instead of a book, you have a course, an online course or you have a salesman who will drop by your house and explain everything to you. All those are vehicles for the same manner of delivering the benefits to you that the features are merely, it's like nobody says, hey, if you call us now, a salesman will call and explain it all to you. That's features. That's not a benefit. Nobody wants a salesman to come over to the house and explain it to them. However, if you can say, if you get involved in this program, we will hold your hand while we explain everything that needs to be done to get this particular benefit done. You will be happier, wealthier. You'll look the way you want to look. You'll look 10 years younger. All of this happens. Those are the benefits that happens. Now, how do we get there? Somebody comes by your house, they hold your hand, they go through the program and blah, blah, blah. You work from there. Yeah, exactly. That's another way you've explained to me before and also is like if a pill could, like you would just as soon take the pill and if we take your seminar exam, because I think that's a good one. You would have taken the pill and then learned how to write ads. Since that is not currently an option, all of the ways you deliver that ability to the avatar who wants that, those are almost certainly all going to be features. The person didn't necessarily, they didn't open their eyes that morning thinking, I want to go to a seminar in Chicago. Let me go find one. Oh, look, here's one that happens to be in something I'm interested in. I'll go to this one. They wanted the benefit you were provided to their lives, to their business and they are willing to deal with the features. The features are, okay, in order to have that information or this service in my life, this is the mechanism by which you're going to make the case this is a good mechanism or these are the right mechanisms by which to have this benefit in your life. Okay, again, this is time you just, it's time to take out a pencil and paper or just start getting the ideas out of your head. I recommend that two column format and then you kind of just loop through it again. You just, okay, the test, if that really is a benefit by applying the simple so what question or what I call the so what test. And they're off. This. Okay, now it's your turn to write down your features and attach benefits to them. Let's go with five or six. Write down five or six of the features that you have so you can identify the features of the product or the service or whatever the product or service of the client or dealing with. Just write them down. You may wind up at the end of the day in a real ad writing situation to have 50 features or maybe just one feature or maybe a hundred features. I've been in all these different circumstances. However, dig deep, try to get five or six, attach at least one benefit to each feature. Now when you attach the benefit, then do the so what test. Say, say, you know, this one is, it's 75 pounds of whatever the product did you have. Then you say the benefit is that it's weighty enough to not get blown over in a strong wind and say so what? Well, if it doesn't get blown over in a strong wind, then it can be up through all the seasons that you have and you'd never have to take it down and you say, well, so what? Well, you don't need storage space for it. Once you set it up, it's going to be there forever. I'm talking like a 75 pound tent or something, a furniture out back or something. So keep going so what? And then go a little deeper. Each of these are levels that are deeper. As you're explaining to somebody why this is a good thing, why this is something that will help you or help you save money, help you do something better, help you get from the situation you're in now to the better situation you want to be in. So five or six features, attach a single benefit to each one, go deeper on each benefit if you can, and go as deep as you want. Keep asking yourself so what? What does this mean? Put yourself in a situation actually explaining to someone sitting across from you that this is the benefit. This is what's so good about it. And they say, so what? Now get inside their head. When you can stop saying that, you've probably gone deep enough.
